title: Acid-Base & Acid-Carbonate Reactions
subtitle: Why some antacids fizz and others don't
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: Investigate the products of reactions involving acids — including acid-base neutralisation and acid-carbonate reactions — and predict products by writing balanced chemical equations
lessonIntent: Students can predict the products of acid-base and acid-carbonate reactions, and explain why carbonates produce CO₂ gas while hydroxides do not.
examSkill: Identify the salt produced in a neutralisation reaction, write balanced equations for acid-base and acid-carbonate reactions including state symbols, and write the universal net ionic equation for strong acid-strong base neutralisation.
productionNotes: Mirrors site lesson `subjects/chemistry/year11/module3/lesson05.html`. Real-world anchor: antacid formulations — Mg(OH)2 (hydroxide, smooth) vs CaCO3 (carbonate, fizz/burp). Worked example 1: H2SO4 + KOH (verbatim from site). Worked example 2: HCl + Na2CO3 (SWC). Misconception: 'a base must contain OH-' (Bronsted-Lowry: any proton acceptor — NH3, Na2CO3 are bases). Universal net ionic for strong acid+base: H+ + OH- → H2O.

scenes.caption: Acid-Base and Acid-Carbonate Reactions — Year 11 Chemistry, Module 3.

voiceover.text: Acid-Base and Acid-Carbonate Reactions. Year eleven Chemistry, Module three.

scenes.heading: Why does one antacid burp and the other doesn't?
scenes.body: You reach for an antacid after a big meal. Some brands fizz and make you burp — TUMS, Rennie, anything with calcium carbonate. Others sit quietly — Mylanta, with magnesium hydroxide. Same problem (excess stomach acid). Same outcome (relief). Different chemistry — and the difference is one product.
scenes.callout: Carbonate antacids release CO₂. Hydroxide antacids don't.
scenes.caption: Carbonate vs hydroxide. The fizz comes from CO₂ — only one of them makes it.

voiceover.text: Here's a question that gets to the heart of this lesson. Why does one antacid burp and the other doesn't? Reach for TUMS or Rennie after a big meal and you fizz — they contain calcium carbonate. Reach for Mylanta and you don't — it contains magnesium hydroxide. Same problem: excess stomach acid. Same outcome: relief. The chemistry is different — and the difference is just one product. Today's lesson teaches you to predict that product every time.

scenes.heading: Two reactions, two product sets
scenes.body: Acids react with two important kinds of base in HSC. The products always follow a fixed pattern, depending on which kind of base it is.

bullets.text: Acid + base (hydroxide or oxide) → salt + water.
Acid + carbonate → salt + water + CO₂ gas.
The salt's cation comes from the base. The anion comes from the acid.
Carbonates always release CO₂ — that's the fizz.

scenes.caption: Two product patterns. The carbonate one always includes CO₂.

voiceover.text: Two reactions, two product sets. Acid plus base — meaning a hydroxide or an oxide — gives a salt plus water. That's neutralisation. Acid plus carbonate gives a salt plus water plus carbon dioxide gas. The cation in the salt always comes from the base. The anion always comes from the acid. So HCl plus NaOH gives sodium chloride: sodium from the base, chloride from the acid. And carbonates always release CO two — that's the fizz you see when an antacid tablet drops into water.

scenes.heading: SWC — the carbonate mnemonic
scenes.body: For acid-carbonate reactions, three products always appear. SWC: Salt, Water, Carbon dioxide. Forgetting any one of them costs marks.

bullets.text: S — Salt: cation from carbonate, anion from acid.
W — Water: H from acid + O from carbonate.
C — Carbon dioxide gas: always evolved as fizz.
Universal net ionic for strong acid+base: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O.

scenes.caption: SWC: Salt + Water + CO₂. Strong acid + base net ionic: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O.

voiceover.text: SWC is the mnemonic. For every acid-carbonate reaction, three products appear: Salt, Water, and Carbon dioxide. Forgetting any one of them costs you a mark. The salt comes from the cation in the carbonate and the anion in the acid. Water comes from the hydrogen in the acid plus oxygen in the carbonate. CO two is always evolved as a gas — that's the fizz. And here's a bonus rule: the universal net ionic equation for any strong acid plus strong base is H plus plus OH minus gives H two O. The same energy is released per mole of water formed, regardless of which acid or base.

scenes.heading: Why pharmacists pick one over the other
scenes.body: Mg(OH)₂ neutralises acid smoothly with no gas. CaCO₃ neutralises acid effectively but releases CO₂ — patients burp. For a hospital bedridden patient who can't sit up, the burp from CaCO₃ becomes a problem. Like choosing between two cars: both get you home, but one has a sunroof you sometimes don't want open.
scenes.callout: Choose hydroxide for no-fizz. Carbonate for fast effervescence.
scenes.caption: Hydroxide antacids = smooth. Carbonate antacids = effervescent.

voiceover.text: Why pharmacists pick one over the other. Magnesium hydroxide neutralises stomach acid smoothly, with no gas evolved. Calcium carbonate neutralises just as effectively, but releases CO two — patients burp. For a hospital patient who can't sit up, that burp becomes a problem. Think of it like choosing between two cars. Both get you home — but one has a sunroof you sometimes don't want open. But unlike a sunroof, you can't close the carbonate's CO two production once the reaction starts.

scenes.heading: Acid-carbonate reaction
scenes.question: A student adds excess hydrochloric acid to sodium carbonate powder (Na₂CO₃). Write the balanced equation with state symbols, identify the three products, and describe two indicators of chemical change.
scenes.coachNote: Target: SWC products. Salt = cation from carbonate + anion from acid. Then balance.
steps: Identify the salt: Na⁺ from Na₂CO₃ + Cl⁻ from HCl → NaCl.
Apply SWC: products are NaCl + H₂O + CO₂.
Formula unbalanced: HCl(aq) + Na₂CO₃(s) → NaCl(aq) + H₂O(l) + CO₂(g).
Substitute coefficients: 2HCl needed for 2 Na (and the H₂O). Balance: 2HCl(aq) + Na₂CO₃(s) → 2NaCl(aq) + H₂O(l) + CO₂(g).
Answer = 2HCl(aq) + Na₂CO₃(s) → 2NaCl(aq) + H₂O(l) + CO₂(g). Indicators: gas evolved (CO₂ fizz) and solid disappearing (Na₂CO₃ consumed).
scenes.caption: 2HCl(aq) + Na₂CO₃(s) → 2NaCl(aq) + H₂O(l) + CO₂(g). SWC products.

voiceover.text: Excess hydrochloric acid added to sodium carbonate. Target: balanced equation, three products, two indicators. Identify the salt: sodium comes from sodium carbonate, chloride comes from HCl, salt is sodium chloride. Apply SWC: products are NaCl, H two O, CO two. Unbalanced equation: HCl plus Na two CO three gives NaCl plus H two O plus CO two. We need two NaCl on the right because Na two CO three has two sodiums — and that means two HCl on the left. Final answer: two HCl plus Na two CO three gives two NaCl plus water plus CO two gas. Indicators: gas evolved as fizz, and the carbonate solid disappearing.

scenes.heading: "A base must contain OH⁻"
scenes.body: Students often think: "To be a base, a compound must have hydroxide ions." That's the wrong instinct from the Arrhenius definition. The actual broader Brønsted-Lowry definition says: a base is any proton acceptor. Instead of looking for OH⁻, look for whether the species can accept a hydrogen ion. NH₃ (ammonia) is a base — it accepts H⁺ to form NH₄⁺. Na₂CO₃ is a base — its CO₃²⁻ accepts H⁺. Oxide ions (O²⁻) are bases. The Brønsted-Lowry definition is the one HSC examiners apply.
scenes.callout: A base accepts a proton. OH⁻ is one example, not the only one.
scenes.caption: A base is any proton acceptor — NH₃, Na₂CO₃, O²⁻ all qualify.

voiceover.text: The trap. Students often think: to be a base, a compound must contain hydroxide ions. That's the wrong instinct, inherited from the older Arrhenius definition. The actual broader definition — Brønsted-Lowry — says a base is any proton acceptor. Instead of looking for an OH minus, ask whether the species can accept a hydrogen ion. Ammonia is a base — it accepts H plus to form NH four plus, even though it has no hydroxide. Sodium carbonate is a base — its carbonate ion accepts H plus. Oxide ions are bases. HSC examiners apply Brønsted-Lowry, not Arrhenius.

scenes.heading: TUMS in vinegar
scenes.body: Drop a TUMS tablet (calcium carbonate) into vinegar (acetic acid). Vigorous fizzing — CO₂ escaping. The carbonate is consumed. Salt + water + CO₂ — SWC in real time.
scenes.caption: Vinegar + TUMS = SWC: calcium acetate + water + CO₂.

voiceover.text: SWC in real time. Drop a TUMS tablet — calcium carbonate — into vinegar, which is acetic acid. Vigorous fizzing as CO two escapes. The tablet shrinks. The salt formed is calcium acetate. Three products, all from one reaction: salt, water, carbon dioxide. The fizz is the most obvious indicator of chemical change here. The same reaction happens in your stomach when you take a TUMS — the burp is the CO two.

scenes.heading: Your turn
scenes.question: A patient takes magnesium hydroxide Mg(OH)₂(s) for stomach acid (HCl). Predict the products, write the balanced equation with states, and explain in one sentence why the patient does NOT burp.
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause: identify reaction type, find the salt, balance, then explain.
answerSteps: Identify type: acid + base (hydroxide) → salt + water (NOT a carbonate, so no SWC).
Identify the salt: Mg²⁺ from Mg(OH)₂ + Cl⁻ from HCl → MgCl₂.
Balance: 2 HCl needed (2 Cl needed); 2 H₂O needed (4 H total). 2HCl(aq) + Mg(OH)₂(s) → MgCl₂(aq) + 2H₂O(l).
Answer = no CO₂ produced. Mg(OH)₂ is a hydroxide, not a carbonate — no fizz, no burp.
scenes.caption: 2HCl(aq) + Mg(OH)₂(s) → MgCl₂(aq) + 2H₂O(l). No CO₂ — no burp.

voiceover.text: Your turn. A patient takes magnesium hydroxide for stomach acid. Predict the products, balance the equation, and explain why there's no burp. Pause now and try before the answer plays. This is acid plus a hydroxide base — neutralisation, not SWC. Salt: magnesium chloride. Magnesium hydroxide has two hydroxide groups, so we need two HCl on the left and two H two O on the right. Final equation: two HCl plus Mg of OH twice gives MgCl two plus two H two O. No CO two produced anywhere. Magnesium hydroxide is a hydroxide, not a carbonate — that's why the patient doesn't burp.

scenes.heading: Summary
scenes.finalPrompt: Ask: is the base a carbonate? If yes — expect CO₂. If no — just salt + water.
points: Acid + base (hydroxide/oxide) → salt + water. Acid + carbonate → SWC (salt + water + CO₂).
Salt's cation comes from the base; anion comes from the acid.
Universal net ionic for strong acid + strong base: H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l).
A base is any proton acceptor (Brønsted-Lowry) — not just compounds with OH⁻.
Carbonate antacids fizz; hydroxide antacids don't. Same job, different chemistry.
scenes.caption: Two reactions. SWC for carbonate. Decision rule, every time.

voiceover.text: Five things to remember. Acid plus a base — hydroxide or oxide — gives salt plus water. Acid plus carbonate gives SWC: salt, water, and carbon dioxide. The salt's cation comes from the base; anion from the acid. The universal net ionic for strong acid plus strong base is H plus plus OH minus gives water. A base is any proton acceptor — that's Brønsted-Lowry, not just compounds with hydroxide. Carbonate antacids fizz; hydroxide antacids don't. Decision rule for the exam: ask if the base is a carbonate. If yes, expect CO two. If no, just salt plus water.
